name: Landing Page
summary: Landing page patterns that communicate value, build trust, and drive conversions.

patterns.name: Hero section
patterns.description: The first screen a visitor sees. Must communicate what the product does, who it's for, and why they should care — in under 5 seconds. One primary CTA.
do: Clear headline that states the benefit (not the feature)
Subheadline that adds specificity (who it's for, how it works)
One primary CTA with high-contrast styling
Product screenshot, video, or illustration that shows value
Keep it above the fold — no scrolling required to understand
dont: Jargon-heavy headline that requires industry knowledge
Headline about the company instead of the user's benefit
Generic stock photos that don't relate to the product
patterns.evidence: Users form an opinion about a page in 50ms. Hero sections with product screenshots convert 80% better than stock photos. Single-CTA heroes outperform multi-CTA by 20%.

patterns.name: Social proof section
patterns.description: Customer logos, testimonials, case studies, ratings, and usage metrics that build trust. Should appear early — ideally as the second section after the hero.
do: Show recognizable customer logos (6-8 is optimal)
Include testimonials with real names, photos, and titles
Display specific metrics ('Saved 10 hours/week', not 'Saved time')
Show aggregate stats ('50,000+ teams trust us')
Place social proof near CTAs to reduce purchase anxiety
dont: Place all social proof at the bottom of the page
Show ratings without the number of reviews
patterns.evidence: 92% of consumers trust peer recommendations over advertising. Testimonials with photos increase trust by 35%. Customer logos increase B2B conversion by 15%.

patterns.name: Feature/benefit blocks
patterns.description: Sections that explain key features through the lens of user benefits. Typically 3-4 features with alternating left/right image+text layout or icon-based grid.
do: Lead with the benefit, follow with the feature
Use screenshots or illustrations showing the feature in action
Keep to 3-4 key features — don't list everything
Use alternating layouts to maintain visual interest
Each feature gets its own clear section with breathing room
dont: Use generic icons that don't relate to the feature
Write feature-first copy ('We have X' vs 'You can do X')
Cram all features into one section
patterns.evidence: Benefit-oriented copy outperforms feature-oriented copy by 20-30%. Limiting to 3-4 features increases recall by 40%.

patterns.name: Final CTA section
patterns.description: A closing section that restates the value proposition and presents the final call to action. This is the 'end' in Peak-End Rule — make it compelling.
do: Restate the core value proposition (different wording from hero)
Use a high-contrast CTA button
Address the primary objection ('No credit card required', 'Free for teams under 10')
Create urgency without manipulation (limited beta, growing community)
Make it visually distinct from the rest of the page
dont: Just repeat the hero section verbatim
Add a lengthy form — keep it to one button
Introduce new information that should have been earlier
End with a generic 'Contact us' instead of a clear action
patterns.evidence: Pages with a final CTA section have 15% higher conversion than those ending with footer content. Peak-End Rule: the last impression disproportionately affects memory.

checklist: Does the hero clearly communicate what the product does in 5 seconds?
Is there one clear primary CTA above the fold?
Is there social proof (logos, testimonials) early on the page?
Are features presented as benefits to the user?
Is there a product screenshot or demo showing real value?
Does the page follow a clear narrative arc (problem → solution → proof → action)?
Is the page responsive and fast-loading on mobile?
Is there a final CTA section before the footer?
Are objections addressed near the CTA ('Free trial', 'No credit card')?
Is the page focused on one goal (not a homepage trying to do everything)?
Does the page load in under 3 seconds?
Is there a clear visual hierarchy guiding the user's eye?
